Gianmarco Tamberi of Italy and Mutaz Barshim of Qatar were both perfect in the high jump until the bar was set to the Olympic-record height of 2.39 meters.
They both missed three times.
Instead of a jump-off, they decided to share the gold.

After they decided, Tamberi slapped Barshim’s hand and jumped into his arms.
“Sharing with a friend is even more beautiful. It was just magical,” Tamberi said

During the deadly heat wave in Pacific Northwest in late June, about 600 more people died than would have been typical, a New York Times analysis shows.
The number is three times as high as states' official estimates of heat-related deaths so far.

Washington State officially reported 95 people died from heat-related causes during the heat wave. But there were nearly 450 excess deaths reported to the CDC.
More people there died during the week of the heat wave than during any other single week of the coronavirus pandemic.
There were nearly 160 excess deaths in Oregon, which has a little more than half the population of its northern neighbor. That's compared to 96 heat-related deaths confirmed by the state.

Officials in British Columbia reported more than 400 heat-related deaths during the week of the heat wave — a stunning number closer to the excess-death estimate for Washington State.
The province's coroner service said the number of excess deaths in the period was even higher.

But deaths are only one measure of the heat wave's impact on health. Though heat-related deaths disproportionately affect older people, high temperatures can make people of all ages sick and lead to hospitalizations.

Here's how emergency department visits in the Pacific Northwest spiked during the heat wave.
On June 28th alone, more than 1,000 heat-related visits to emergency departments were reported, compared with fewer than 10 visits during the same period in 2019.

"Not everyone can stay at home all day in an air-conditioned space," Dr. Greg Wellenius of Boston University said. "Not everyone can afford an air conditioner."
Read more about what we're learning of the true toll from June's heat wave.

The U.S. has a far higher share of seniors without full vaccine protection than many other wealthy countries, a New York Times analysis shows.
Even as vaccinations pick up, many older Americans are deeply vulnerable.

The high rate of unvaccinated older Americans is a key risk factor driving serious Covid-19 illness and death.
In many counties, especially in the South and Mountain West, seniors without full vaccination make up more than 10% of the total population. https://nyti.ms/2UMPMJS

By contrast, unvaccinated seniors in Britain — along with Spain and Canada — are relatively rare.
Those nations also have far fewer disparities between the most and least vaccinated areas, according to local immunization data. https://nyti.ms/2UMPMJS

The discrepancy in how many older people are unvaccinated may help explain why the Delta wave has led to such a higher rate of death in the U.S. than in Britain, public health experts say. https://nyti.ms/2UMPMJS

Although coronavirus cases surged in both countries because of the Delta variant, there were many fewer hospitalizations and deaths in Britain than in the U.S., suggesting that vaccines had weakened the link between infection and serious disease. https://nyti.ms/2UMPMJS
